mnemonics.title: FRIEND记忆法
mnemonics.content:
F - Function not a member
R - Receives objects as parameters
I - Inheritance doesn't apply
E - External to the class
N - Not transitive
D - Doesn't have 'this' pointer

acronyms.expansion: Function with Right to access Internal and External Non-member Data
acronyms.usage: Helps remember that friend functions have special access to private data without being members

visualTips.visualization: Draw a box representing the class. Place 'friend' keyword inside with an arrow pointing to an external function outside the box, showing it's not a member but has special access (shown as a dashed line crossing the class boundary)
Two overlapping circles where one class circle has an arrow pointing to the other, with a lock icon showing private members are accessible between them

analogies.analogy: Think of a friend function like a trusted contractor who has a key to your house (class) to do specific work. They're not family members (not part of the class), but they have special access privileges that regular visitors (regular functions) don't have.

If Alice trusts Bob (Alice makes Bob her friend), and Bob trusts Charlie, it doesn't mean Alice automatically trusts Charlie. Similarly in C++, friendship granted to one class doesn't extend to other classes.

A member function is like your own hand - it naturally has access to everything in your body. A friend function is like a doctor's prescription - it has special access to help you but is not part of your body.
